risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "A statistician at the University of California Davis stratified individual communities by selected indicators ... and computer-generated an assignment within strata to participate in the intervention cohort (IC; 25 communities) or in the non-intervention cohort (NIC; 9 communities). The same statistician, who was blinded to the intervention, generated a random allocation sequence at the level of the concession for the enrollment of eligible infants in the IC." Comment: The study explicitly describes computer-generated assignment within strata and a random allocation sequence generated by a statistician, which indicates a proper random sequence generation method.

Quote: "A statistician at the University of California Davis stratified individual communities by selected indicators ... and computer-generated an assignment within strata... The same statistician, who was blinded to the intervention, generated a random allocation sequence at the level of the concession for the enrollment of eligible infants in the IC." Comment: This describes who generated the random sequence and that the statistician was blinded, but it does not explicitly describe any allocation concealment mechanism preventing recruiters/enrollers from foreseeing upcoming assignments (e.g. central allocation, SNOSE, pharmacy-controlled system). In this cluster trial, the report also does not clearly state whether participant recruitment occurred before or after cluster/concession randomization in a way that rules out foreknowledge by recruiters.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The trial was partially masked, as all participants, field staff and researchers remained blinded to the four intervention groups until data analyses were completed, but were aware which communities were assigned to IC and NIC." Quote: "Children in the intervention groups received free treatment for diarrhea, malaria and fever... Children in the NIC did not receive SQ-LNS or tablets from 9 to 18 months of age, nor any illness treatment..." Comment: Participants and personnel were not blinded to intervention versus non-intervention cohort assignment. Because important outcomes included subjective or behavior-sensitive outcomes such as caregiver-reported diarrhea, reported fever, and care-seeking/treatment-related morbidity surveillance, knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence outcomes and co-interventions. Although masking existed within the four intervention groups, the overall study included an unblinded IC vs NIC comparison that could affect dominant outcomes.

Quote: "The trial was partially masked, as all participants, field staff and researchers remained blinded to the four intervention groups until data analyses were completed, but were aware which communities were assigned to IC and NIC." Quote: "Children in the IC were visited weekly for delivery of intervention products and morbidity surveillance using standardized data collection tools." Quote: "Diarrhea was defined as the presence of ≥3 loose or liquid stools per 24 hr. Reported fever was defined as any fever reported by the caregiver." Comment: Outcome assessors included patient/caregiver self-report (for diarrhea and reported fever) and field staff/clinician judgement for morbidity surveillance. Because participants/caregivers and field staff were explicitly aware of IC versus NIC allocation, and the study included subjective/judgement-dependent outcomes based on caregiver report, knowledge of allocation could bias outcome measurement. By the worst-outcome rule, these unblinded subjective morbidity outcomes make the domain high risk overall, despite other objective outcomes (e.g. Hb, pZn, anthropometry, mortality).

Quote: "Among the four intervention groups, 19.5% dropped out by the final visit at 18 months. In the NIC, 15.2% were lost to anthropometric follow up at 18 months. Caregivers of 97.1% of children in the intervention groups provided morbidity surveillance information for at least 30 days and 78% provided information for at least 35 weeks, with no difference in participation rates among the four intervention groups." Quote: Table 5 reports morbidity analysis denominators: randomized N = 602, 613, 603, 617 (Table 2) and analysed N = 579, 593, 593, 599 for diarrhea/malaria. Comment: For the review-relevant morbidity outcomes actually reported (diarrhea and malaria), post-randomization loss on the analysed outcome was low and balanced across the 4 randomized intervention arms: 23/602 (3.8%), 20/613 (3.3%), 10/603 (1.7%), and 18/617 (2.9%). This is essentially complete follow-up for those analysed outcomes with no meaningful imbalance. Although anthropometric follow-up loss by 18 months was higher, the relevant morbidity outcome denominators are reported and do not indicate problematic attrition bias.
